# kill_probes.txt — the probe corpus for the cleanup-ending guard (SPEC INV-162, ROADMAP 417).
#
# Each line is `VERDICT | command`, VERDICT one of RED (the guard must red it) or PASS (the guard
# must let it through quiet). The corpus is the fixture the guard's own test drives, so a later
# widening of the guard cannot silently NARROW what it catches: a probe dropped from RED here is a
# visible edit, not an invisible regression. The guard reads a command's OWNED-IDENTITY proof, not a
# name list — so the corpus names programs the pack never launches (Safari, Slack) beside ones it does
# (chrome, node), and the guard reds them all the same, because none proves what it owns.
#
# blank lines and #-comment lines are ignored.

# --- RED: an ending that names a NAME, with no PID / process-group / owned-path proof ---
RED | killall Safari
RED | pkill -f node
RED | killall -9 Electron
RED | pkill firefox
RED | pkill -f Slack
RED | pkill chrome
RED | pkill -9 chrome
RED | pkill -9 -f "chrome_crashpad_handler"
RED | killall Chrome
RED | killall "Google Chrome"
RED | kill -9 $(pgrep chrome)
RED | pgrep chrome | xargs kill -9
RED | kill $(pidof node)
RED | ps aux | grep -i chrome | awk '{print $2}' | xargs kill -9
RED | ps aux | grep 'Google Chrome' | awk '{print $2}' | xargs kill
RED | /usr/bin/pkill chrome

# --- RED: a shared install path is NOT a safe target (SPEC INV-162, ROADMAP 335). A kill by
# ~/.cache/puppeteer or user-data-dir reaches other sessions' live browsers, so only the recorded
# process group stays inside this run. These once passed on a path exemption the spec forbids; the
# adversarial review of 2026-07-17 flipped them. ---
RED | pkill -9 -f "$HOME/.cache/puppeteer/chrome/.*chrome"
RED | pkill -f user-data-dir

# --- PASS: an ending that proves what it owns (a recorded PID or a process group) ---
PASS | os.killpg(self.proc.pid, signal.SIGKILL)
PASS | kill "$pid"
PASS | kill -9 "$OWNER_PID"
PASS | kill 48213
PASS | os.kill(pid, 0)
PASS | pgrep chrome  # just list them, no kill verb
